goal: Refine structured signal writes into separate constraint risk and followup rules

summary.title: Enforce structured signal write rules
summary.what: Refined the explicit structured-signal implementation so seal submit no longer uses one broad opt-in gate. Constraints are rejected from seal, risks use structured objects with failure mode plus trigger or impact and mitigation, validation, or owner, and follow-ups use structured objects with explicit_defer, external_reference, or cut_scope provenance. Legacy string risks and follow-ups still read for historical ledgers.
summary.why: The previous PR only stopped default writes but still allowed agents to bypass the policy with one flag. The important behavior is entry control: when an agent writes a signal, the type-specific rule must be enforced by code and by the prompt contract.
summary.user_goal: Refine structured signal writes into separate constraint risk and followup rules

decisions.chose: Reject summary.anti_patterns from CLI seal submit under the structured signal policy.
decisions.rationale: Constraints are future-agent rules and must be human-promoted through an interactive path rather than authored by an agent seal.

decisions.chose: Replace new risk prose with RiskStatement objects while keeping legacy string JSON readable.
decisions.rationale: The validator can now require failure_mode, trigger or impact, and mitigation, validation, or owner without losing old ledger compatibility.

decisions.chose: Replace new follow-up prose with FollowupStatement objects carrying explicit_defer, external_reference, or cut_scope provenance.
decisions.rationale: Follow-ups should record real deferred work with auditable source, not agent-invented maybe-later backlog.

decisions.chose: Remove --allow-structured-signals from CLI/docs/templates and teach the three distinct rules instead.
decisions.rationale: A broad flag taught agents the wrong mental model; the prompt contract must match the code gate.

rejected.alternative: Keep --allow-structured-signals and rely on prompt discipline
rejected.reason: That is still a single bypass valve and does not enforce the constraint/risk/followup split the product needs.

rejected.alternative: Make risks and followups interactive-only too
rejected.reason: The chosen policy allows agents to write these lower-authority signals when they are structured and auditable.

review_notes: Targeted tests passed for domain signal validation, seal submit enforcement, affected materializers, templates, hub, storage, eval, and CLI compile path.

architectural_claims: Structured signal write authority is now type-specific: constraints are not seal-authored, risks are validated present-review warnings, and follow-ups require auditable provenance.
Legacy risk/follow-up string arrays remain readable through compatibility unmarshalling and text projection helpers.
behavioral_changes: CLI seal submit enforces structured signal write rules instead of offering --allow-structured-signals.
summary.risks accepts structured RiskStatement objects and rejects incomplete agent-authored risks under CLI policy.
summary.followups accepts structured FollowupStatement objects and rejects speculative sources under CLI policy.
summary.anti_patterns is rejected by CLI seal submit under the structured signal policy.
PR, Hub, context retrieval, storage cache, eval fixtures, and lint paths render legacy and structured risk/follow-up text through helper methods.

data_model_changes.name: IntentSummary.Risks
data_model_changes.migration_notes: Old JSON string risks unmarshal into RiskStatement with LegacyText.

data_model_changes.name: IntentSummary.Followups
data_model_changes.migration_notes: Old JSON string follow-ups unmarshal into FollowupStatement with LegacyText.

migration_notes: Existing actor-log events with string risks or follow-ups remain readable.
New CLI seal submissions must use structured risk/follow-up objects and cannot create anti_patterns.
